obj.content: a reference to a type of tomato that can resist a dangerous infection
obj.html_content: a reference to a type of tomato that can resist a dangerous infection

obj.content: an explanation of how problems can arise from focusing only on a certain type of tomato plant.
obj.html_content: an explanation of how problems can arise from focusing only on a certain type of tomato plant.

obj.content: a number of examples of plants that are not cultivated at present but could be useful as food sources
obj.html_content: a number of examples of plants that are not cultivated at present but could be useful as food sources

obj.title: A second attempt at domesticating the tomato
obj.content:
[br/]
[p:a]It took at least 3,000 years for humans to learn how to domesticate the wild tomato and cultivate it for food. Now two separate teams in Brazil and China have done it all over again in less than three years. And they have done it better in some ways, as the re-domesticated tomatoes are more nutritious than the ones we eat at present. [br/][br/]
This approach relies on the revolutionary CRISPR genome editing technique, in which changes are deliberately made to the DNA of a living cell, allowing genetic material to be added, removed or altered. The technique could not only improve existing crops, but could also be used to turn thousands of wild plants into useful and appealing foods. In fact, a third team in the US has already begun to do this with a relative of the tomato called the groundcherry. [br/][br/]
This fast-track domestication could help make the world's food supply healthier and far more resistant to diseases, such as the rust fungus devastating wheat crops. [br/][br/]
'This could transform what we eat,' says Jorg Kudla at the University of Munster in Germany, a member of the Brazilian team. 'There are 50,000 edible plants in the world, but 90 percent of our energy comes from just 15 crops.' [br/][br/]
'We can now mimic the known domestication course of major crops like rice, maize, sorghum or others,' says Caixia Gao of the Chinese Academy of Sciences in Beijing. 'Then we might try to domesticate plants that have never been domesticated.'[br/][br/]
[p:b]Wild tomatoes, which are native to the Andes region in South America, produce pea-sized fruits. Over many generations, peoples such as the Aztecs and Incas transformed the plant by selecting and breeding plants with mutations* in their genetic structure, which resulted in desirable traits such as larger fruit. [br/][br/]
But every time a single plant with a mutation is taken from a larger population for breeding, much genetic diversity is lost. And sometimes the desirable mutations come with less desirable traits. For instance, the tomato strains grown for supermarkets have lost much of their flavour. [br/][br/]
By comparing the genomes of modern plants to those of their wild relatives, biologists have been working out what genetic changes occurred as plants were domesticated. The teams in Brazil and China have now used this knowledge to reintroduce these changes from scratch while maintaining or even enhancing the desirable traits of wild strains. [br/][br/]
[p:c]Kudla's team made six changes altogether. For instance, they tripled the size of fruit by editing a gene called FRUIT WEIGHT, and increased the number of tomatoes per truss by editing another called MULTIFLORA. [br/][br/]
While the historical domestication of tomatoes reduced levels of the red pigment lycopene – thought to have potential health benefits – the team in Brazil managed to boost it instead. The wild tomato has twice as much lycopene as cultivated ones; the newly domesticated one has five times as much. [br/][br/]
'They are quite tasty,' says Kudla. 'A little bit strong. And very aromatic.' [br/][br/]
The team in China re-domesticated several strains of wild tomatoes with desirable traits lost in domesticated tomatoes. In this way they managed to create a strain resistant to a common disease called bacterial spot race, which can devastate yields. They also created another strain that is more salt tolerant – and has higher levels of vitamin C. [br/][br/]
[p:d]Meanwhile, Joyce Van Eck at the Boyce Thompson Institute in New York state decided to use the same approach to domesticate the groundcherry or goldenberry (Physalis pruinosa) for the first time. This fruit looks similar to the closely related Cape gooseberry (Physalis peruviana). [br/][br/]
Groundcherries are already sold to a limited extent in the US but they are hard to produce because the plant has a sprawling growth habit and the small fruits fall off the branches when ripe. Van Eck's team has edited the plants to increase fruit size, make their growth more compact and to stop fruits dropping. 'There's potential for this to be a commercial crop,' says Van Eck. But she adds that taking the work further would be expensive because of the need to pay for a licence for the CRISPR technology and get regulatory approval. [br/][br/]
[p:e]This approach could boost the use of many obscure plants, says Jonathan Jones of the Sainsbury Lab in the UK. But it will be hard for new foods to grow so popular with farmers and consumers that they become new staple crops, he thinks. [br/][br/]
The three teams already have their eye on other plants that could be 'catapulted into the mainstream', including foxtail, oat-grass and cowpea. By choosing wild plants that are drought or heat tolerant, says Gao, we could create crops that will thrive even as the planet warms. [br/][br/]
But Kudla didn't want to reveal which species were in his team's sights, because CRISPR has made the process so easy. 'Any one with the right skills could go to their lab and do this.'
obj.content_zh:
[br/][b]A.[/b]人类花了至少 3000 年的时间才学会如何驯化野生番茄并将其栽培成食物。现在，巴西和中国的两个团队在不到三年的时间里又重新完成了这项工作。而且他们在某些方面做得更好，因为重新驯化的番茄比我们现在吃的番茄更有营养。[br/]
这种方法依靠的是革命性的 CRISPR 基因组编辑技术，即故意改变活细胞的 DNA，从而添加、删除或改变遗传物质。这种技术不仅可以改良现有作物，还可以用来把成千上万种野生植物变成有用和有吸引力的食物。事实上，美国的第三个研究小组已经开始对番茄的近亲 “地衣 ”进行这项研究。[br/]
这种快速的驯化可以帮助世界粮食供应变得更加健康，对疾病的抵抗力也大大增强，例如对小麦作物造成破坏的锈病真菌。[br/]
巴西研究小组成员、德国明斯特大学的约尔格-库德拉（Jorg Kudla）说："这将改变我们的饮食习惯。世界上有 5 万种可食用植物，但我们 90% 的能量仅来自 15 种作物。[br/]
北京中国科学院的高彩霞说：'我们现在可以模仿已知的水稻、玉米、高粱等主要作物的驯化过程。然后，我们可能会尝试驯化从未被驯化过的植物。[br/]
[b]B.[/b]野生番茄原产于南美洲的安第斯山脉地区，结出的果实只有豌豆大小。经过许多代人的努力，阿兹台克人和印加人等民族通过选择和培育基因结构发生突变*的植物来改造这种植物，从而使其具有更大的果实等理想性状。[br/]
但是，每当从更大的种群中选育出一株有突变的植物时，就会失去很多遗传多样性。有时，理想的突变会带来不那么理想的性状。例如，为超市培育的西红柿品系已经失去了很多风味。[br/]
通过比较现代植物的基因组与其野生近缘植物的基因组，生物学家一直在研究植物被驯化后发生了哪些基因变化。现在，巴西和中国的研究小组利用这些知识，从头开始重新引入这些变化，同时保持甚至增强野生品系的理想特性。[br/]
[b]C.[/b]库德拉的团队一共进行了六次修改。例如，他们通过编辑一个名为 “果实重量”（FRUIT WEIGHT）的基因，使果实的大小增加了两倍；通过编辑另一个名为 “多花序”（MULTIFLORA）的基因，增加了每桁架上西红柿的数量。[br/]
历史上番茄的驯化降低了红色素番茄红素的含量，而巴西的研究小组却设法提高了番茄红素的含量。野生番茄的番茄红素含量是栽培番茄的两倍，而新驯化番茄的番茄红素含量是栽培番茄的五倍。[br/]
它们相当美味，"库德拉说。'味道有点浓。而且非常芳香。[br/]
中国的研究小组重新驯化了几个野生番茄品系，这些品系具有驯化番茄所失去的理想性状。通过这种方法，他们成功地培育出了一种对细菌性斑点病这种常见病具有抗性的品系，这种病会破坏产量。他们还培育出了另一种耐盐性更强、维生素 C 含量更高的品系。[br/]
[b]D.[/b]与此同时，纽约州博伊斯-汤普森研究所的乔伊斯-范-埃克（Joyce Van Eck）决定使用同样的方法首次驯化地莓或金莓（Physalis pruinosa）。这种水果看起来与近缘的开普鹅莓（Physalis peruviana）相似。[br/]
美国已经在一定程度上出售地莓，但由于这种植物的生长习性蔓生，成熟时小果实会从枝头掉落，因此很难种植。Van Eck 的团队对植物进行了改良，以增大果实大小，使其生长更加紧凑，并阻止果实掉落。Van Eck 说："这有可能成为一种商业作物。但她补充说，进一步开展这项工作将耗资巨大，因为需要支付CRISPR技术的许可费并获得监管部门的批准。[br/]
[b]E.[/b]英国塞恩斯伯里实验室的乔纳森-琼斯（Jonathan Jones）说，这种方法可以促进许多不起眼植物的利用。但他认为，新食品很难受到农民和消费者的欢迎，以至于成为新的主要作物。[br/]
这三个团队已经盯上了其他可能 “跃居主流 ”的植物，包括狐尾草、燕麦草和豇豆。高说，通过选择耐旱或耐热的野生植物，我们可以创造出即使地球变暖也能茁壮成长的作物。[br/]
但库德拉并不想透露他的团队看中了哪些物种，因为CRISPR技术让这一过程变得如此简单。任何拥有适当技能的人都可以去实验室做这件事。
obj.html_content:
aIt took at least 3,000 years for humans to learn how to domesticate the wild tomato and cultivate it for food. Now two separate teams in Brazil and China have done it all over again in less than three years. And they have done it better in some ways, as the re-domesticated tomatoes are more nutritious than the ones we eat at present. 



This approach relies on the revolutionary CRISPR genome editing technique, in which changes are deliberately made to the DNA of a living cell, allowing genetic material to be added, removed or altered. The technique could not only improve existing crops, but could also be used to turn thousands of wild plants into useful and appealing foods. In fact, a third team in the US has already begun to do this with a relative of the tomato called the groundcherry. 



This fast-track domestication could help make the world's food supply healthier and far more resistant to diseases, such as the rust fungus devastating wheat crops. 



'This could transform what we eat,' says Jorg Kudla at the University of Munster in Germany, a member of the Brazilian team. 'There are 50,000 edible plants in the world, but 90 percent of our energy comes from just 15 crops.' 



'We can now mimic the known domestication course of major crops like rice, maize, sorghum or others,' says Caixia Gao of the Chinese Academy of Sciences in Beijing. 'Then we might try to domesticate plants that have never been domesticated.'



bWild tomatoes, which are native to the Andes region in South America, produce pea-sized fruits. Over many generations, peoples such as the Aztecs and Incas transformed the plant by selecting and breeding plants with mutations* in their genetic structure, which resulted in desirable traits such as larger fruit. 



But every time a single plant with a mutation is taken from a larger population for breeding, much genetic diversity is lost. And sometimes the desirable mutations come with less desirable traits. For instance, the tomato strains grown for supermarkets have lost much of their flavour. 



By comparing the genomes of modern plants to those of their wild relatives, biologists have been working out what genetic changes occurred as plants were domesticated. The teams in Brazil and China have now used this knowledge to reintroduce these changes from scratch while maintaining or even enhancing the desirable traits of wild strains. 



cKudla's team made six changes altogether. For instance, they tripled the size of fruit by editing a gene called FRUIT WEIGHT, and increased the number of tomatoes per truss by editing another called MULTIFLORA. 



While the historical domestication of tomatoes reduced levels of the red pigment lycopene – thought to have potential health benefits – the team in Brazil managed to boost it instead. The wild tomato has twice as much lycopene as cultivated ones; the newly domesticated one has five times as much. 



'They are quite tasty,' says Kudla. 'A little bit strong. And very aromatic.' 



The team in China re-domesticated several strains of wild tomatoes with desirable traits lost in domesticated tomatoes. In this way they managed to create a strain resistant to a common disease called bacterial spot race, which can devastate yields. They also created another strain that is more salt tolerant – and has higher levels of vitamin C. 



dMeanwhile, Joyce Van Eck at the Boyce Thompson Institute in New York state decided to use the same approach to domesticate the groundcherry or goldenberry (Physalis pruinosa) for the first time. This fruit looks similar to the closely related Cape gooseberry (Physalis peruviana). 



Groundcherries are already sold to a limited extent in the US but they are hard to produce because the plant has a sprawling growth habit and the small fruits fall off the branches when ripe. Van Eck's team has edited the plants to increase fruit size, make their growth more compact and to stop fruits dropping. 'There's potential for this to be a commercial crop,' says Van Eck. But she adds that taking the work further would be expensive because of the need to pay for a licence for the CRISPR technology and get regulatory approval. 



eThis approach could boost the use of many obscure plants, says Jonathan Jones of the Sainsbury Lab in the UK. But it will be hard for new foods to grow so popular with farmers and consumers that they become new staple crops, he thinks. 



The three teams already have their eye on other plants that could be 'catapulted into the mainstream', including foxtail, oat-grass and cowpea. By choosing wild plants that are drought or heat tolerant, says Gao, we could create crops that will thrive even as the planet warms. 



But Kudla didn't want to reveal which species were in his team's sights, because CRISPR has made the process so easy. 'Any one with the right skills could go to their lab and do this.'

added_content.ex_information:
Look at the following statements (Questions 19-23) and the list of researchers below.
Match each statement with the correct researcher,
A-D
.
Write the correct letter,
A-D
, in boxes 19-23 on your answer sheet.
NB
You may use any letter more than once.

obj.content: Domestication of certain plants could allow them to adapt to future environmental challenges. [match]19[/match]
obj.html_content: Domestication of certain plants could allow them to adapt to future environmental challenges. 19

obj.content: The idea of growing and eating unusual plants may not be accepted on a large scale [match]20[/match]
obj.html_content: The idea of growing and eating unusual plants may not be accepted on a large scale 20

obj.content: It is not advisable for the future direction of certain research to be made public. [match]21[/match]
obj.html_content: It is not advisable for the future direction of certain research to be made public. 21

obj.content: Present efforts to domesticate one wild fruit are limited by the costs involved. [match]22[/match]
obj.html_content: Present efforts to domesticate one wild fruit are limited by the costs involved. 22

obj.content: Humans only make use of a small proportion of the plant food available on Earth. [match]23[/match]
obj.html_content: Humans only make use of a small proportion of the plant food available on Earth. 23

obj.content_zh:
[br/][b]A.[/b]人类花了至少 3000 年的时间才学会如何驯化野生番茄并将其栽培成食物。现在，巴西和中国的两个团队在不到三年的时间里又重新完成了这项工作。而且他们在某些方面做得更好，因为重新驯化的番茄比我们现在吃的番茄更有营养。[br/]

中国的研究小组重新驯化了几个野生番茄品系，这些品系具有驯化番茄所失去的理想性状。通过这种方法，他们成功地培育出了一种对细菌性斑点病这种常见病具有抗性的品系，这种病会破坏产量。他们还培育出了另一种耐盐性更强、维生素 C 含量更高的品系。[br/]
[b]D.[/b]与此同时，纽约州博伊斯-汤普森研究所的乔伊斯-范-埃克（Joyce Van Eck）决定使用同样的方法首次驯化地莓或金莓（Physalis pruinosa）。这种水果看起来与近缘的开普鹅莓（Physalis peruviana）相似。[br/]
美国已经在一定程度上出售地莓，但由于这种植物的生长习性蔓生，成熟时小果实会从枝头掉落，因此很难种植。Van Eck 的团队对植物进行了改良，以增大果实大小，使其生长更加紧凑，并阻止果实掉落。Van Eck 说："这有可能成为一种商业作物。但她补充说，进一步开展这项工作将耗资巨大，因为需要支付CRISPR技术的许可费并获得监管部门的批准。[br/]
[b]E.[/b]英国塞恩斯伯里实验室的乔纳森-琼斯（Jonathan Jones）说，这种方法可以促进许多不起眼植物的利用。但他认为，新食品很难受到农民和消费者的欢迎，以至于成为新的主要作物。[br/]
这三个团队已经盯上了其他可能 “跃居主流 ”的植物，包括狐尾草、燕麦草和豇豆。高说，通过选择耐旱或耐热的野生植物，我们可以创造出即使地球变暖也能茁壮成长的作物。[br/]
但库德拉并不想透露他的团队看中了哪些物种，因为CRISPR技术让这一过程变得如此简单。任何拥有适当技能的人都可以去实验室做这件事。
obj.html_content:
aIt took at least 3,000 years for humans to learn how to domesticate the wild tomato and cultivate it for food. Now two separate teams in Brazil and China have done it all over again in less than three years. And they have done it better in some ways, as the re-domesticated tomatoes are more nutritious than the ones we eat at present. 



This approach relies on the revolutionary CRISPR genome editing technique, in which changes are deliberately made to the DNA of a living cell, allowing genetic material to be added, removed or altered. The technique could not only improve existing crops, but could also be used to turn thousands of wild plants into useful and appealing foods. In fact, a third team in the US has already begun to do this with a relative of the tomato called the groundcherry. 



This fast-track domestication could help make the world's food supply healthier and far more resistant to diseases, such as the rust fungus devastating wheat crops. 



'This could transform what we eat,' says Jorg Kudla at the University of Munster in Germany, a member of the Brazilian team. 'There are 50,000 edible plants in the world, but 90 percent of our energy comes from just 15 crops.' 



'We can now mimic the known domestication course of major crops like rice, maize, sorghum or others,' says Caixia Gao of the Chinese Academy of Sciences in Beijing. 'Then we might try to domesticate plants that have never been domesticated.'



bWild tomatoes, which are native to the Andes region in South America, produce pea-sized fruits. Over many generations, peoples such as the Aztecs and Incas transformed the plant by selecting and breeding plants with mutations* in their genetic structure, which resulted in desirable traits such as larger fruit. 



But every time a single plant with a mutation is taken from a larger population for breeding, much genetic diversity is lost. And sometimes the desirable mutations come with less desirable traits. For instance, the tomato strains grown for supermarkets have lost much of their flavour. 



By comparing the genomes of modern plants to those of their wild relatives, biologists have been working out what genetic changes occurred as plants were domesticated. The teams in Brazil and China have now used this knowledge to reintroduce these changes from scratch while maintaining or even enhancing the desirable traits of wild strains. 



cKudla's team made six changes altogether. For instance, they tripled the size of fruit by editing a gene called FRUIT WEIGHT, and increased the number of tomatoes per truss by editing another called MULTIFLORA. 



While the historical domestication of tomatoes reduced levels of the red pigment lycopene – thought to have potential health benefits – the team in Brazil managed to boost it instead. The wild tomato has twice as much lycopene as cultivated ones; the newly domesticated one has five times as much. 



'They are quite tasty,' says Kudla. 'A little bit strong. And very aromatic.' 



The team in China re-domesticated several strains of wild tomatoes with desirable traits lost in domesticated tomatoes. In this way they managed to create a strain resistant to a common disease called bacterial spot race, which can devastate yields. They also created another strain that is more salt tolerant – and has higher levels of vitamin C. 



dMeanwhile, Joyce Van Eck at the Boyce Thompson Institute in New York state decided to use the same approach to domesticate the groundcherry or goldenberry (Physalis pruinosa) for the first time. This fruit looks similar to the closely related Cape gooseberry (Physalis peruviana). 



Groundcherries are already sold to a limited extent in the US but they are hard to produce because the plant has a sprawling growth habit and the small fruits fall off the branches when ripe. Van Eck's team has edited the plants to increase fruit size, make their growth more compact and to stop fruits dropping. 'There's potential for this to be a commercial crop,' says Van Eck. But she adds that taking the work further would be expensive because of the need to pay for a licence for the CRISPR technology and get regulatory approval. 



eThis approach could boost the use of many obscure plants, says Jonathan Jones of the Sainsbury Lab in the UK. But it will be hard for new foods to grow so popular with farmers and consumers that they become new staple crops, he thinks. 



The three teams already have their eye on other plants that could be 'catapulted into the mainstream', including foxtail, oat-grass and cowpea. By choosing wild plants that are drought or heat tolerant, says Gao, we could create crops that will thrive even as the planet warms. 



But Kudla didn't want to reveal which species were in his team's sights, because CRISPR has made the process so easy. 'Any one with the right skills could go to their lab and do this.'

added_content.ex_information:
Complete the sentences below.
Choose
ONE WORD ONLY
from the passage for each answer.
Write your answers in boxes 24-26 on your answer sheet.

obj.content: An undesirable trait such as loss of [blank]24[/blank] may be caused by a mutation in a tomato gene.
obj.html_content: An undesirable trait such as loss of may be caused by a mutation in a tomato gene.

obj.content: By modifying one gene in a tomato plant, researchers made the tomato three times its original [blank]25[/blank].
obj.html_content: By modifying one gene in a tomato plant, researchers made the tomato three times its original .

obj.content: A type of tomato which was not badly affected by [blank]26[/blank], and was rich in vitamin C, was produced by a team of researchers in China.
obj.html_content: A type of tomato which was not badly affected by , and was rich in vitamin C, was produced by a team of researchers in China.

obj.title: A second attempt at domesticating the tomato
obj.content:
[br/]
[p:a]It took at least 3,000 years for humans to learn how to domesticate the wild tomato and cultivate it for food. Now two separate teams in Brazil and China have done it all over again in less than three years. And they have done it better in some ways, as the re-domesticated tomatoes are more nutritious than the ones we eat at present. [br/][br/]
This approach relies on the revolutionary CRISPR genome editing technique, in which changes are deliberately made to the DNA of a living cell, allowing genetic material to be added, removed or altered. The technique could not only improve existing crops, but could also be used to turn thousands of wild plants into useful and appealing foods. In fact, a third team in the US has already begun to do this with a relative of the tomato called the groundcherry. [br/][br/]
This fast-track domestication could help make the world's food supply healthier and far more resistant to diseases, such as the rust fungus devastating wheat crops. [br/][br/]
'This could transform what we eat,' says Jorg Kudla at the University of Munster in Germany, a member of the Brazilian team. 'There are 50,000 edible plants in the world, but 90 percent of our energy comes from just 15 crops.' [br/][br/]
'We can now mimic the known domestication course of major crops like rice, maize, sorghum or others,' says Caixia Gao of the Chinese Academy of Sciences in Beijing. 'Then we might try to domesticate plants that have never been domesticated.'[br/][br/]
[p:b]Wild tomatoes, which are native to the Andes region in South America, produce pea-sized fruits. Over many generations, peoples such as the Aztecs and Incas transformed the plant by selecting and breeding plants with mutations* in their genetic structure, which resulted in desirable traits such as larger fruit. [br/][br/]
But every time a single plant with a mutation is taken from a larger population for breeding, much genetic diversity is lost. And sometimes the desirable mutations come with less desirable traits. For instance, the tomato strains grown for supermarkets have lost much of their flavour. [br/][br/]
By comparing the genomes of modern plants to those of their wild relatives, biologists have been working out what genetic changes occurred as plants were domesticated. The teams in Brazil and China have now used this knowledge to reintroduce these changes from scratch while maintaining or even enhancing the desirable traits of wild strains. [br/][br/]
[p:c]Kudla's team made six changes altogether. For instance, they tripled the size of fruit by editing a gene called FRUIT WEIGHT, and increased the number of tomatoes per truss by editing another called MULTIFLORA. [br/][br/]
While the historical domestication of tomatoes reduced levels of the red pigment lycopene – thought to have potential health benefits – the team in Brazil managed to boost it instead. The wild tomato has twice as much lycopene as cultivated ones; the newly domesticated one has five times as much. [br/][br/]
'They are quite tasty,' says Kudla. 'A little bit strong. And very aromatic.' [br/][br/]
The team in China re-domesticated several strains of wild tomatoes with desirable traits lost in domesticated tomatoes. In this way they managed to create a strain resistant to a common disease called bacterial spot race, which can devastate yields. They also created another strain that is more salt tolerant – and has higher levels of vitamin C. [br/][br/]
[p:d]Meanwhile, Joyce Van Eck at the Boyce Thompson Institute in New York state decided to use the same approach to domesticate the groundcherry or goldenberry (Physalis pruinosa) for the first time. This fruit looks similar to the closely related Cape gooseberry (Physalis peruviana). [br/][br/]
Groundcherries are already sold to a limited extent in the US but they are hard to produce because the plant has a sprawling growth habit and the small fruits fall off the branches when ripe. Van Eck's team has edited the plants to increase fruit size, make their growth more compact and to stop fruits dropping. 'There's potential for this to be a commercial crop,' says Van Eck. But she adds that taking the work further would be expensive because of the need to pay for a licence for the CRISPR technology and get regulatory approval. [br/][br/]
[p:e]This approach could boost the use of many obscure plants, says Jonathan Jones of the Sainsbury Lab in the UK. But it will be hard for new foods to grow so popular with farmers and consumers that they become new staple crops, he thinks. [br/][br/]
The three teams already have their eye on other plants that could be 'catapulted into the mainstream', including foxtail, oat-grass and cowpea. By choosing wild plants that are drought or heat tolerant, says Gao, we could create crops that will thrive even as the planet warms. [br/][br/]
But Kudla didn't want to reveal which species were in his team's sights, because CRISPR has made the process so easy. 'Any one with the right skills could go to their lab and do this.'
obj.content_zh:
[br/][b]A.[/b]人类花了至少 3000 年的时间才学会如何驯化野生番茄并将其栽培成食物。现在，巴西和中国的两个团队在不到三年的时间里又重新完成了这项工作。而且他们在某些方面做得更好，因为重新驯化的番茄比我们现在吃的番茄更有营养。[br/]
这种方法依靠的是革命性的 CRISPR 基因组编辑技术，即故意改变活细胞的 DNA，从而添加、删除或改变遗传物质。这种技术不仅可以改良现有作物，还可以用来把成千上万种野生植物变成有用和有吸引力的食物。事实上，美国的第三个研究小组已经开始对番茄的近亲 “地衣 ”进行这项研究。[br/]
这种快速的驯化可以帮助世界粮食供应变得更加健康，对疾病的抵抗力也大大增强，例如对小麦作物造成破坏的锈病真菌。[br/]
巴西研究小组成员、德国明斯特大学的约尔格-库德拉（Jorg Kudla）说："这将改变我们的饮食习惯。世界上有 5 万种可食用植物，但我们 90% 的能量仅来自 15 种作物。[br/]
北京中国科学院的高彩霞说：'我们现在可以模仿已知的水稻、玉米、高粱等主要作物的驯化过程。然后，我们可能会尝试驯化从未被驯化过的植物。[br/]
[b]B.[/b]野生番茄原产于南美洲的安第斯山脉地区，结出的果实只有豌豆大小。经过许多代人的努力，阿兹台克人和印加人等民族通过选择和培育基因结构发生突变*的植物来改造这种植物，从而使其具有更大的果实等理想性状。[br/]
但是，每当从更大的种群中选育出一株有突变的植物时，就会失去很多遗传多样性。有时，理想的突变会带来不那么理想的性状。例如，为超市培育的西红柿品系已经失去了很多风味。[br/]
通过比较现代植物的基因组与其野生近缘植物的基因组，生物学家一直在研究植物被驯化后发生了哪些基因变化。现在，巴西和中国的研究小组利用这些知识，从头开始重新引入这些变化，同时保持甚至增强野生品系的理想特性。[br/]
[b]C.[/b]库德拉的团队一共进行了六次修改。例如，他们通过编辑一个名为 “果实重量”（FRUIT WEIGHT）的基因，使果实的大小增加了两倍；通过编辑另一个名为 “多花序”（MULTIFLORA）的基因，增加了每桁架上西红柿的数量。[br/]
历史上番茄的驯化降低了红色素番茄红素的含量，而巴西的研究小组却设法提高了番茄红素的含量。野生番茄的番茄红素含量是栽培番茄的两倍，而新驯化番茄的番茄红素含量是栽培番茄的五倍。[br/]
它们相当美味，"库德拉说。'味道有点浓。而且非常芳香。[br/]
中国的研究小组重新驯化了几个野生番茄品系，这些品系具有驯化番茄所失去的理想性状。通过这种方法，他们成功地培育出了一种对细菌性斑点病这种常见病具有抗性的品系，这种病会破坏产量。他们还培育出了另一种耐盐性更强、维生素 C 含量更高的品系。[br/]
[b]D.[/b]与此同时，纽约州博伊斯-汤普森研究所的乔伊斯-范-埃克（Joyce Van Eck）决定使用同样的方法首次驯化地莓或金莓（Physalis pruinosa）。这种水果看起来与近缘的开普鹅莓（Physalis peruviana）相似。[br/]
美国已经在一定程度上出售地莓，但由于这种植物的生长习性蔓生，成熟时小果实会从枝头掉落，因此很难种植。Van Eck 的团队对植物进行了改良，以增大果实大小，使其生长更加紧凑，并阻止果实掉落。Van Eck 说："这有可能成为一种商业作物。但她补充说，进一步开展这项工作将耗资巨大，因为需要支付CRISPR技术的许可费并获得监管部门的批准。[br/]
[b]E.[/b]英国塞恩斯伯里实验室的乔纳森-琼斯（Jonathan Jones）说，这种方法可以促进许多不起眼植物的利用。但他认为，新食品很难受到农民和消费者的欢迎，以至于成为新的主要作物。[br/]
这三个团队已经盯上了其他可能 “跃居主流 ”的植物，包括狐尾草、燕麦草和豇豆。高说，通过选择耐旱或耐热的野生植物，我们可以创造出即使地球变暖也能茁壮成长的作物。[br/]
但库德拉并不想透露他的团队看中了哪些物种，因为CRISPR技术让这一过程变得如此简单。任何拥有适当技能的人都可以去实验室做这件事。
obj.html_content:
aIt took at least 3,000 years for humans to learn how to domesticate the wild tomato and cultivate it for food. Now two separate teams in Brazil and China have done it all over again in less than three years. And they have done it better in some ways, as the re-domesticated tomatoes are more nutritious than the ones we eat at present. 



This approach relies on the revolutionary CRISPR genome editing technique, in which changes are deliberately made to the DNA of a living cell, allowing genetic material to be added, removed or altered. The technique could not only improve existing crops, but could also be used to turn thousands of wild plants into useful and appealing foods. In fact, a third team in the US has already begun to do this with a relative of the tomato called the groundcherry. 



This fast-track domestication could help make the world's food supply healthier and far more resistant to diseases, such as the rust fungus devastating wheat crops. 



'This could transform what we eat,' says Jorg Kudla at the University of Munster in Germany, a member of the Brazilian team. 'There are 50,000 edible plants in the world, but 90 percent of our energy comes from just 15 crops.' 



'We can now mimic the known domestication course of major crops like rice, maize, sorghum or others,' says Caixia Gao of the Chinese Academy of Sciences in Beijing. 'Then we might try to domesticate plants that have never been domesticated.'



bWild tomatoes, which are native to the Andes region in South America, produce pea-sized fruits. Over many generations, peoples such as the Aztecs and Incas transformed the plant by selecting and breeding plants with mutations* in their genetic structure, which resulted in desirable traits such as larger fruit. 



But every time a single plant with a mutation is taken from a larger population for breeding, much genetic diversity is lost. And sometimes the desirable mutations come with less desirable traits. For instance, the tomato strains grown for supermarkets have lost much of their flavour. 



By comparing the genomes of modern plants to those of their wild relatives, biologists have been working out what genetic changes occurred as plants were domesticated. The teams in Brazil and China have now used this knowledge to reintroduce these changes from scratch while maintaining or even enhancing the desirable traits of wild strains. 



cKudla's team made six changes altogether. For instance, they tripled the size of fruit by editing a gene called FRUIT WEIGHT, and increased the number of tomatoes per truss by editing another called MULTIFLORA. 



While the historical domestication of tomatoes reduced levels of the red pigment lycopene – thought to have potential health benefits – the team in Brazil managed to boost it instead. The wild tomato has twice as much lycopene as cultivated ones; the newly domesticated one has five times as much. 



'They are quite tasty,' says Kudla. 'A little bit strong. And very aromatic.' 



The team in China re-domesticated several strains of wild tomatoes with desirable traits lost in domesticated tomatoes. In this way they managed to create a strain resistant to a common disease called bacterial spot race, which can devastate yields. They also created another strain that is more salt tolerant – and has higher levels of vitamin C. 



dMeanwhile, Joyce Van Eck at the Boyce Thompson Institute in New York state decided to use the same approach to domesticate the groundcherry or goldenberry (Physalis pruinosa) for the first time. This fruit looks similar to the closely related Cape gooseberry (Physalis peruviana). 



Groundcherries are already sold to a limited extent in the US but they are hard to produce because the plant has a sprawling growth habit and the small fruits fall off the branches when ripe. Van Eck's team has edited the plants to increase fruit size, make their growth more compact and to stop fruits dropping. 'There's potential for this to be a commercial crop,' says Van Eck. But she adds that taking the work further would be expensive because of the need to pay for a licence for the CRISPR technology and get regulatory approval. 



eThis approach could boost the use of many obscure plants, says Jonathan Jones of the Sainsbury Lab in the UK. But it will be hard for new foods to grow so popular with farmers and consumers that they become new staple crops, he thinks. 



The three teams already have their eye on other plants that could be 'catapulted into the mainstream', including foxtail, oat-grass and cowpea. By choosing wild plants that are drought or heat tolerant, says Gao, we could create crops that will thrive even as the planet warms. 



But Kudla didn't want to reveal which species were in his team's sights, because CRISPR has made the process so easy. 'Any one with the right skills could go to their lab and do this.'

sectionSheet.title: A second attempt at domesticating the tomato

officialExplain.explain: 当诸如reference、account、mention这类词汇出现时，它们表达的意思都是“提到、说到”，考生无需花费过多时间关注这些词。本题干意思清楚，其中表达主要意思的resist和dangerous也是再基础不过的词汇了，只有infection也许对一些考生来说稍显陌生，但这其实也是一个在以往剑桥真题阅读文章中高频出现的认知词汇。题干所说的某种类型的西红柿可以抵抗“某种危险的感染”，原文中直接给出了这种感染疾病的名称：bacterial spot race；虽然这个疾病名称显得陌生，但句子里还使用了common disease和can devastate yields这样的描述说明，足以帮助考生进行对应和选择。

本题干中没有使用任何高难度词汇，唯一的困难也许来自于题干本身较长，需要考生首先去掉an explanation of这个没有实际意义的表达，再对how引导的句子中的几个介词短语各归各位、看懂意思即可。要重点寻找的信息有两个意群，一个是“可能出现问题”，另一个则是出现问题的原因即“只专注某种番茄”。Section B第二段的三句话正是在表达这些内容：第一句话说单独选出一种变异品种可能会导致基因多样性的遗失；第二句话说虽然选出来的这个突变品种可能有一些好处，然而也往往同时有一些坏处。至此，已经给出了两个专注某品种所造成的问题。第三句话以举例的方式进一步说明了第二句话的信息：比如在超市售卖的番茄品种都没什么西红柿味。至此，看懂这三句话大意的考生应该已经能够作出正确选择了。

本题的题干虽然长，但却并不是一个句子，其本身就值得做个语法分析：主体是名词词组a number of examples of plants，后面有两部分修饰内容，一部分是完整定语从句that are not cultivated at present，另一部分则是共用了主语that、因此省略了主语的谓宾结构could be useful as food sources。考生一旦看懂了题干本身，应该就不难匹配到Section E中的对应位置。该部分第二段第一句尤其明显地做了提示，给出了一系列具体的植物名称。考生即使并不认识这都是些什么植物名字（毕竟在第一段里已经被定性为obscure plants了），应该也不难看出它们与题干的匹配对应关系。
本题之所以简单，大概是因为其出处来自全文开篇第一段。即使是未能读完全文的考生，看懂第一段里的比较（第一次人工培植番茄花了至少三千年，而如今进行二次改造只用了不到三年）也不困难，进而得出正确答案。
只要阅读了Section C第三段这总共一行的三句内容，考生就应该不难看出，此处说的正是Kudla其人对味道的点评描述。再结合前文一直在说的都是人工编辑改良后的新番茄品种，就可以非常笃定地完成匹配答题。
本题题干的阅读理解难度不高，但是定位并不容易，这主要是因为其出处非常靠后，来自于全文倒数第二段；另一方面，本题的解题难度也不算高，因为对应原文中的drought和heat都是雅思阅读中的高频核心词汇，用来同义替换题干中的environmental challenges；原文中的thrive也是如此，对应题干中的adapt to。因此本题答案为B。

本题的特点与上一题类似：题干本身和对应的原文不难看懂，因为都基本没有太陌生的词汇；即使考生不认识第一句里的boost和obscure，通过读完了两个句子的大意以后，其实也能根据第二句的跟进说明，大致猜测出obscure plants应该就是以前没怎么见过的、第二句里所说的“新食物”，由此得出其与题干unusual plants的对应。唯一的解题难点在于题干的对应原文比较靠后。但换一个角度来看，如果考生在之前的阅读里一直稳扎稳打地进行了平行阅读，则在进入Section E的阅读时应当很是振奋：因为之前的文本里一直没有本题型中19~22这四道题，可知答案必然是集中在了这个Section。耐心阅读可知本题的正确答案为D。
本题的难度仍然更多来自于出处位置，而非阅读理解句意本身。在直到最后一个自然段之前的所有文章篇幅里，都不曾出现题干所说的“不要公开”这个含义，于是最后一段里Kudla所说的“不想透露信息”就必然是对题干的同义替换，考生可以据此确定正确选项为A。
要做对本题，考生只需耐心顺序阅读文章，在本段前几句中不难看出，这种称之为groundberries的植物应该是野生水果且目前并没有大批量生产销售；而最后一句中的would be expensive则更是直接对应题干的cost。考生应该不怎么费力就可以准确匹配到正确选项为C。
题干中的proportion是雅思阅读文章中多次出现的核心认知词汇，还没有背下来的考生请立刻牢记住它。题干里的a small proportion在原文中换成了更加具体的数字对比：可食用植物有5万种，而我们人类日常吃的只有15种。看懂了这些直白表达，也就不难对应到正确选项为A。

本题的定位不难，虽然mutation原词在文中的第一次出现是在Section B的第一段里，距离真正的定位处还有几句话的间隔，但显然本题并不能只靠这一个单词来定位；而另一个定位提示，题干的undesirable与对应原文中less desirable的近义替换，也不难看出。考生只需在确定了第二段第二句是真正定位的起点之后，再向下寻找一句内容，即可根据第三句也就是最后一句中的lost这个信息锁定答案为flavour。
本题本质上其实就是在考查一个基础认知词汇：triple。认识这个单词的考生便可轻松在原文中定位到题干three times的相应出处，进而锁定答案为size。

本题从定位到解题都非常简单。可以直接依靠vitamin C这个信息定位到文中相应位置。分析题干可知，这种番茄需要具备两种品质，一种是富含维生素C，另一种是不受什么的太大影响，也就是空格中所缺少的信息。这两种品质会是并列出现在句子中的。利用这种并列关系，考生可以轻松锁定that is more salt tolerant这个部分，再根据tolerant这个高频认知词汇的意思，进而确定答案为salt。
